henry-s-digital-canvas.whyItMatters: This portfolio is the public proof surface for the consulting work itself, so the presentation layer, runtime evidence, and editorial workflow all have to stay aligned.
problem: The site needed to present consulting work as evidence-backed case studies instead of a flat gallery of repository links.
Proof points had to stay easy to refresh as repository metadata, WordPress content, and project narratives changed over time.
A public marketing surface still needed real runtime and release discipline behind the presentation layer.
approach: Built the front end as a React 19 + Vite 7 SPA driven by structured repository, page, and resume data.
Added page-law manifests, route-specific laws, and a repository check script so page changes keep the intended structure and evidence model intact.
Used a Cloudflare Worker layer to proxy GitHub proof endpoints, front WordPress and contact routes, and keep operational logic outside the browser.
Expanded the Work and Work Detail flows around curated case-study data, code-tour quick look, and contrast-gated presentation checks.
result: Work pages now read like maintained case studies with receipts, architecture notes, and code tours instead of a raw repository dump.
Page-level changes stay safer because the repo enforces page-law and contrast checks alongside lint, tests, and build validation.
The live portfolio has a dedicated runtime layer for GitHub proofs, WordPress content, and Turnstile-backed form handling.
highlights: Cloudflare Worker proof and contact runtime

receipts.label: Runtime layer
receipts.value: The Worker fronts health, contact, WordPress, and GitHub proof routes behind the portfolio UI.

receipts.label: Editorial guardrails
receipts.value: Page-law resolution and the route check script keep page-specific structure and source files in sync.

receipts.label: Visual QA
receipts.value: A dedicated contrast workflow checks page probes so presentation drift is caught before the site ships.

architecture.summary: React 19 + Vite 7 portfolio SPA backed by a Cloudflare Worker and page-law guardrails.
bullets: Structured repository and content data power the home, work, detail, resume, and blog surfaces instead of hard-coded page fragments.
Page laws define route-specific constraints and are enforced by repository scripts so layout changes do not silently drift from intent.
The Cloudflare Worker handles GitHub proofs, WordPress and blog endpoints, contact submission, and health checks behind the public SPA.

keyFiles.note: Work showcase page that turns curated metadata into browseable case-study surfaces.
Repository detail page that turns project data into narrative sections, receipts, and code-tour surfaces.
Worker entry point for health, contact, WordPress, blog, and GitHub proof routes.
GitHub proof routes that feed releases, language, contributor, and file-preview evidence into the work surfaces.
Route-law verification script that keeps page-source changes aligned with the editorial ruleset.

henry-s-digital-canvas.shipped: Added design-system guardrails with automated contrast auditing, token-drift detection, and page-law enforcement across all portfolio routes.
henry-s-digital-canvas.learned: Treating a portfolio like a product surface made the docs, data model, runtime endpoints, and presentation rules reinforce each other instead of drift apart.
henry-s-digital-canvas.accessNote: Source is private. Available for walkthrough on request.

azure_chatapp.whyItMatters: Shows a fuller Azure OpenAI app shape than a single chat form by combining auth, project scoping, knowledge routes, and telemetry wiring in one repo.
problem: Needed a way to tie conversations and knowledge actions to individual users instead of anonymous prompts.
The app had to support project-scoped chat flows and related knowledge utilities from the same backend.
The repo also needed a consistent bootstrap story for structured logging and Sentry-style telemetry.
approach: Implemented JWT auth and user session plumbing around the main chat experience.
Built backend routes for unified conversations and related project knowledge actions instead of a single completion endpoint.
Centralized structured logging and Sentry initialization in a shared bootstrap module.
result: Users can sign in and work inside scoped chat and knowledge flows rather than an anonymous demo.
The codebase has clearer operational setup because telemetry bootstraps in one place.
The repository still reads as a development-oriented baseline, with explicit warnings about insecure or debug posture in parts of the stack.
highlights: JWT auth around Azure OpenAI routes

receipts.label: Telemetry bootstrap
receipts.value: One bootstrap module initializes structured logging first and then configures Sentry with environment-aware sampling.

receipts.label: Auth and conversation wiring
receipts.value: The app entrypoint and conversation routes tie JWT auth to persisted, project-scoped chat flows.

architecture.summary: Python web app that wraps Azure OpenAI chat behind JWT auth, scoped routes, and shared telemetry initialization.
bullets: Auth middleware and session plumbing gate access to chat and knowledge endpoints.
Conversation routes handle project-scoped chat flows and persistence concerns on the backend.
Bootstrap utilities initialize structured logging and Sentry before the rest of the app starts.

keyFiles.note: Application entrypoint that wires middleware, routes, and runtime safeguards together.
Auth layer for JWT handling and user access control around the chat experience.
Conversation route module for scoped chat and related project interactions.
Single bootstrap path for structured logging and Sentry configuration.

azure_chatapp.shipped: Built a JWT-authenticated Azure OpenAI chat baseline with scoped conversation routes and telemetry wiring.
azure_chatapp.learned: The repo reinforced that auth, route design, and observability have to be treated as first-class parts of an AI app, even when the product surface is still rough.

chatter.whyItMatters: Shows how model-specific behavior changes the chat UX: reasoning models, streaming-capable models, and search helpers do not all behave the same way.
problem: Needed a Flask chat surface that could work across several Azure OpenAI model types without pretending they shared the same capabilities.
Users still needed saved sessions, utility helpers like weather and search, and a readable web UI.
Operational logging mattered because the app coordinates chat, uploads, scraping, and model config.
approach: Added a model configuration matrix that records per-model limits and whether streaming is supported.
Built Flask chat and session flows with multi-session history, utility routes, and Azure OpenAI integration.
Layered structured logging and client-event logging across the app to debug model and chat behavior.
result: The app can change delivery behavior by model instead of claiming one-size-fits-all streaming.
o1-preview is handled as a non-streaming reasoning model, while supported models such as o3-mini can stream.
Users still get a multi-session chat experience with utility integrations and persisted state.

receipts.label: Capability guardrails
receipts.value: The model config explicitly marks o1-preview and o1-mini as non-streaming while enabling streaming for supported models like o3-mini.

receipts.label: Operational visibility
receipts.value: Structured logging and client-side event logging are wired through the chat stack for debugging and monitoring.

architecture.summary: Flask chat application with model-specific capability rules, persisted sessions, and utility integrations.
bullets: Model configuration defines required params, token limits, and streaming support per deployment type.
Chat routes and messaging helpers manage session history, prompt delivery, and streamed or non-streamed response handling.
Logging modules capture backend and client events so behavior differences between models are easier to trace.

keyFiles.note: Model capability matrix and configuration rules that drive chat behavior by deployment type.
Messaging logic for chat delivery, streaming behavior, and session flow control.
API surface that exposes the chat functionality and related helper integrations.
Data model for persisted chat state and conversation records.

chatter.shipped: Built a model-aware Flask chat app with session history, utility helpers, and Azure OpenAI integration.
chatter.learned: This repo made the integration constraint obvious: the UX has to match the real model contract, not the marketing shorthand.

real_time_chat_app.whyItMatters: Captures the architecture of an early real-time AI chat experiment, where room messaging and AI replies share the same Socket.IO flow.
problem: Needed chat messages to move instantly between participants instead of relying on repeated page refreshes.
AI replies had to plug into the same room model and persistence layer as human messages.
The server still needed uploads, logging, and database wiring around the realtime core.
approach: Built Flask-SocketIO room messaging around conversations, participants, and stored messages.
Integrated Azure OpenAI response generation inside the async send-message handler.
Added file logging, CORS and session configuration, and SQLAlchemy models to support a fuller app shell.
result: The repo demonstrates the core room-join, message-persist, and AI-reply loop for a realtime chat app.
Conversation threads are stored instead of living only in browser memory.
The codebase is clearly unfinished: multiple files still contain merge markers, so it reads as a rough prototype rather than a finished shipped system.
highlights: Azure OpenAI reply loop inside chat flow

receipts.label: Message loop
receipts.value: The socket handler checks room membership, stores the user message, requests an AI reply, stores it, and emits both sides of the conversation.

receipts.label: Ops scaffolding
receipts.value: The app entrypoint configures file logging, uploads, CORS, and Socket.IO startup around the chat server.

architecture.summary: Prototype Flask-SocketIO chat stack with persisted conversations and Azure OpenAI reply generation.
bullets: HTTP and Socket.IO layers share the same Flask app and SQLAlchemy-backed data model.
Socket handlers manage room membership, persistence, typing state, and AI reply emission.
Application startup wires logging, uploads, CORS, and config loading around the realtime layer.

keyFiles.note: Main Flask application setup for logging, uploads, database init, and Socket.IO wiring.
Realtime message loop that validates participants, stores messages, and emits AI replies.
Conversation route layer that complements the socket-based chat flow.
Azure OpenAI configuration layer used by the realtime assistant flow.

real_time_chat_app.shipped: Built a room-based Socket.IO chat prototype with Azure OpenAI responses and persisted threads.
real_time_chat_app.learned: The repo is a useful reminder that realtime architecture can be sound even when the surrounding product still needs cleanup and merge resolution.

claude-code.whyItMatters: Shows how to move a CLI-centric model workflow into a browser surface without losing streaming, session history, or runtime guardrails.
problem: Claude interactions were easier from a terminal than from a phone or lightweight browser client.
The app needed live streaming and conversation continuity instead of one-shot request and response forms.
Different runtimes had to be supported: direct Anthropic API use and an optional Claude CLI bridge.
approach: Built a mobile-first React and Tailwind UI that connects to a Node and Express backend over WebSockets.
Stored sessions and messages in SQLite so conversation history survives across browser refreshes.
Added provider switching between Anthropic API and an optional Claude CLI runtime, plus lightweight auth and permission defaults.
result: Claude conversations can be run from a browser-first interface instead of only through terminal tooling.
Streaming text and saved sessions make longer chats feel continuous on mobile and desktop.
Prometheus-style metrics and runtime guardrails make the server easier to inspect and safer to extend.

receipts.label: Runtime flexibility
receipts.value: The server supports both direct Anthropic API usage and an optional Claude CLI bridge with permission controls.

receipts.label: Operational signal
receipts.value: Metrics helpers expose HTTP latency, request counters, and WebSocket activity for inspection.

architecture.summary: Browser chat UI backed by a Node service that streams Claude responses, persists sessions in SQLite, and can delegate to either API or CLI runtimes.
bullets: React views handle mobile-first chat layout, settings, and streamed message assembly.
Server runtimes abstract provider choice between Anthropic API calls and the Claude CLI bridge.
SQLite stores sessions and messages while the metrics layer tracks HTTP and WebSocket behavior.

keyFiles.note: Browser chat shell for mobile-first interaction, settings, and streamed response assembly.
Primary runtime bridge for direct Claude API integration.
Optional CLI runtime bridge with permission and tool controls.
SQLite session and message store used to persist chat history.

claude-code.shipped: Built a browser-first Claude chat app with streaming, saved sessions, and an optional CLI bridge.
claude-code.learned: Moving an AI workflow into the browser is mostly about state, runtime boundaries, and guardrails, not just message rendering.

agentic-rag.whyItMatters: Shows the orchestration pieces that make RAG more than a single retrieve-and-answer call: query rewriting, routing, verification, and observability.
problem: Single-pass retrieval struggles on ambiguous or multi-hop questions.
A useful prototype needed to stream agent progress and citations instead of only a final answer.
The stack needed hooks for evaluation and tracing without pretending every future layer was already production-ready.
approach: Built a TypeScript coordinator that classifies queries, optionally rewrites them, retrieves evidence, and loops through verification before final output.
Combined hybrid retrieval, reranking, semantic caching, and server-sent event streams in the backend chat route.
Added OpenTelemetry bootstrap code and instrumentation helpers so the system can emit spans and events as it grows.
result: The prototype can stream tokens, citations, rewrite events, and verification status from the same chat flow.
Local retrieval can be augmented with hybrid search and optional web search when the query calls for it.
The repo documents a large architecture, but the implementation is best read as a serious prototype with extension hooks rather than a finished enterprise platform.
highlights: Verification loops with SSE and OTEL hooks

receipts.label: Coordinator loop
receipts.value: The orchestration coordinator handles caching, query rewriting, retrieval, generation, and bounded verification retries in one flow.

receipts.label: Tracing hook
receipts.value: The OpenTelemetry config boots a Node SDK and exposes span helpers for retrieval and orchestration work.

architecture.summary: TypeScript RAG prototype that layers coordination, retrieval, verification, streaming, and tracing into the chat path.
bullets: Chat routes emit server-sent events for planner logs, tokens, citations, and final answer state.
Coordinator services classify queries, manage rewrite and retrieval decisions, and run bounded verification loops.
Retrieval and observability modules provide hybrid search, reranking, caches, and OpenTelemetry helpers.

keyFiles.note: Chat route that streams orchestration events, tokens, citations, and final responses.
Coordinator logic for query routing, rewrite handling, retrieval decisions, and verification loops.
Retrieval layer for hybrid search and evidence collection.
OpenTelemetry bootstrap and span helpers for runtime tracing.

agentic-rag.shipped: Built a TypeScript RAG prototype with rewrite, retrieval, reranking, verification, and streaming feedback.
agentic-rag.learned: The highest-leverage RAG improvements came from explicit coordination and verification, not from adding more architectural labels.

mobile-rag-app.whyItMatters: Shows what a mobile document assistant can realistically do on-device versus what still depends on remote model calls.
problem: Users needed a phone-friendly way to ingest PDFs, text files, and images into a personal knowledge base.
Mobile storage and retrieval had to stay local and responsive instead of round-tripping every query through a server.
The app still needed OCR, embeddings, chat, and error reporting around model-backed features.
approach: Built an Expo and React Native app that stores documents, chunks, and document centroids in local SQLite tables.
Added local parsing for text and PDFs, plus image OCR and embeddings via remote OpenAI calls.
Wrapped model-dependent code with pacing, retries, and Sentry instrumentation to make mobile failures easier to inspect.
result: Documents and retrieval state live on-device, so the library remains browsable and searchable locally.
PDF, text, and image inputs all feed the same local chunk store and ranking flow.
The product is not fully offline: embeddings, OCR, and chat still rely on network calls to OpenAI services.
highlights: Local SQLite document and chunk store

receipts.label: Local retrieval path
receipts.value: The vector database module stores documents, chunks, and per-document centroids in Expo SQLite for ranked local lookup.

receipts.label: Operational guardrails
receipts.value: Sentry initialization and embedding backoff logic handle mobile model failures more explicitly than a demo-only app.

architecture.summary: React Native document assistant with local SQLite retrieval and remote model calls for embeddings, OCR, and chat.
bullets: Document-processing utilities normalize text, PDF, and image inputs into chunked local records.
SQLite tables store documents, chunks, and centroid indexes used by the ranking path.
API helpers call OpenAI services for embeddings and OCR while Sentry captures runtime failures.

keyFiles.note: Top-level mobile app shell for the document assistant experience.
Local SQLite storage and ranking logic for documents, chunks, and centroids.
Ingestion pipeline for text, PDF, and image assets before they enter local retrieval.
Remote embedding client that powers the local ranking pipeline.
Sentry bootstrap and error-reporting helpers for mobile runtime issues.

mobile-rag-app.shipped: Built a mobile document assistant with local retrieval, multi-format ingestion, and OpenAI-backed AI features.
mobile-rag-app.learned: The biggest design constraint on mobile was being explicit about which capabilities are truly local and which still need networked model calls.

document-qa.whyItMatters: A useful reminder that some AI demos are intentionally tiny: this repo is a minimal upload-and-ask example, not a retrieval system.
problem: Needed the smallest possible interface for asking questions about a local text document.
Non-technical users benefit from a no-install UI more than from a full search stack.
The example had to stay short enough to understand in one file.
approach: Built a single-file Streamlit app that accepts a user API key, uploads a txt or md file, and asks a question.
Injected the full document text directly into a gpt-3.5-turbo chat completion request.
Streamed the model response back through Streamlit for immediate feedback.
result: Small text documents can be queried through a very low-friction UI.
The implementation is easy to read and adapt because the whole flow lives in one file.
It does not do retrieval, citation grounding, or PDF support, so the case study stays intentionally modest.

receipts.label: Supported inputs
receipts.value: The uploader only accepts txt and md files, keeping the example narrow and understandable.

receipts.label: Response path
receipts.value: The app streams a gpt-3.5-turbo completion built from the full uploaded document text and the user's question.

architecture.summary: One-file Streamlit example that uploads a text document and streams a chat completion from its contents.
bullets: Streamlit handles API-key input, file upload, and question entry in a single page.
The app reads the uploaded file directly into memory and appends the user's question.
OpenAI chat completions stream the answer back to the page without a retrieval layer.

keyFiles.note: Single-file implementation of the upload, prompt assembly, and streamed response flow.

document-qa.shipped: Built a minimal Streamlit document-QA starter for small text files.
document-qa.learned: Not every repo should be narrated like a platform; sometimes the right description is simply small, readable starter.

newfolder.whyItMatters: Shows a more grounded document workspace pattern where classic search and chat history are first-class, and vector search is only one optional layer.
problem: Users needed one place to search documents, chat threads, and project context instead of jumping across separate surfaces.
Project-scoped workspaces needed permission-aware search rather than a single global index.
Semantic search had to behave as an optional enhancement instead of the only retrieval path.
approach: Built a FastAPI backend that queries documents and chat messages from the same authenticated search route.
Added optional vector-search augmentation when embeddings and the vector service are configured.
Wired a TypeScript frontend to the document APIs so upload, browsing, and chat tools share the same workspace.
result: Users can search documents and prior chats from one endpoint scoped to their project or account.
Keyword and metadata results work even when vector search is unavailable or unconfigured.
The repo suggests a broader RAG direction, but the strongest, clearest implementation today is the unified workspace and search layer.
highlights: Documents and chats in one workspace

receipts.label: Unified search route
receipts.value: The search endpoint returns document hits, chat hits, and semantic matches from one authenticated request.

receipts.label: Best-effort semantics
receipts.value: Vector search only runs when an OpenAI key and the vector service are available; otherwise the app still returns document and chat results.

architecture.summary: Document workspace with authenticated project search across files, chat history, and optional vector matches.
bullets: FastAPI search routes join project ownership checks with document and chat queries.
Search services combine SQL lookups with optional embedding and vector-store calls when configured.
TypeScript frontend utilities talk to the same workspace APIs for document browsing and chat-adjacent actions.

keyFiles.note: Backend application entrypoint for the authenticated workspace service.
Unified search route across documents, chats, and semantic matches.
Search service that combines SQL lookups with optional embedding-backed retrieval.
TypeScript client utilities for the document workspace APIs.

newfolder.shipped: Built a project-scoped document workspace with unified search across files, chats, and optional semantic results.
newfolder.learned: The repo highlighted that traditional search and workspace boundaries still matter even when you plan to add richer RAG features later.

docs.whyItMatters: Useful as an internal tooling experiment: it explores how provider selection, chunking, and templates can automate repetitive documentation work.
problem: Code docs were inconsistent and easy to postpone across Python repositories.
A single model provider was too limiting for experimentation and cost tradeoffs.
The output needed templates and process management, not just a one-off prompt.
approach: Built a Python CLI that loads provider configs and can target Azure, Gemini, or OpenAI backends.
Added a documentation process manager to walk files, chunk source, call the chosen provider, and collect results.
Used a template manager to render Markdown outputs into consistent shapes.
result: The repo produces a runnable experimentation surface for AI-assisted documentation generation.
Provider configuration, process management, and templating are separated clearly enough to iterate on them independently.
It is still rough: even the README metadata is placeholder-heavy, so this should be presented as experimental tooling rather than a mature product.

receipts.label: Provider routing
receipts.value: The CLI can initialize Azure, Gemini, or OpenAI clients from the same configuration path.

receipts.label: Template layer
receipts.value: The template manager caches Jinja templates for main, function, class, metric, and summary outputs.

architecture.summary: Experimental Python doc-generation pipeline built from provider selection, process management, and Jinja-based templates.
bullets: CLI arguments control repository scope, provider choice, schema, concurrency, and output location.
Process managers walk files, prepare chunks, and coordinate model calls and metrics.
Template rendering assembles generated content into repeatable Markdown structures.

keyFiles.note: CLI entrypoint that selects providers, config, and repository scope for a documentation run.
Language-specific handling for Python source during documentation generation.
Process manager for file traversal, chunking, and provider-call orchestration.
Template cache and renderer for Markdown documentation output.

docs.shipped: Built an experimental AI documentation generator with provider selection and template-driven output.
docs.learned: What mattered most here was decomposition: once provider choice, process management, and templating were separated, the rest of the experimentation became much cheaper.

ai-prompt-pro.whyItMatters: Shows prompt authoring as a real product surface: builder, persistence, community, and agent-backed enhancement all share one system instead of living in separate demos.
problem: Prompt drafting, enhancement, and reuse were scattered across chat sessions, notes, and one-off demos with no durable product surface.
Users needed help turning rough ideas into structured builder fields without losing control of the final prompt.
The app needed a secure path for model-backed enhancement, saved prompts, community sharing, and optional repository context.
approach: Built a React + TypeScript application with dedicated routes for builder, library, history, presets, community feed, user profiles, and support.
Added heuristic inference for role, tone, length, format, and constraints, plus quality scoring before any enhancement call runs.
Backed signed-in state with Neon Postgres via the Neon Data API and Neon Auth, while keeping local persistence for signed-out sessions.
Split the enhancement backend into focused modules for SSE prompt refinement, URL extraction, builder-field inference, and GitHub App-backed repository context.
result: Prompt Forge runs as a production prompt workspace and community feed at prompt.lakefrontdigital.io.
The builder can score, infer, save, remix, and share prompts instead of acting like a one-shot demo.
The enhancement service handles authenticated streaming refinement plus repository-aware context collection for signed-in users.
Release gates now include docs freshness, typed front-end tests, and split frontend-service deployment workflows.
highlights: Structured prompt builder with quality scoring
Community feed, profiles, and remix attribution

receipts.label: Builder state
receipts.value: The main builder hook manages scoring, inferred fields, draft persistence, auth-aware saves, and remix context from one state path.

receipts.label: Repository context
receipts.value: The service exposes GitHub App install, callback, connection, file preview, and context-source routes for signed-in builders.

receipts.label: Release hygiene
receipts.value: The repo includes docs freshness checks and production verification scripts alongside the application build and test commands.

architecture.summary: Route-based prompt workspace and community platform, backed by Neon Postgres and a multi-module enhancement service deployed separately from the frontend.
bullets: Browser routes separate builder, library, community feed, user profiles, history, presets, and support flows with route-specific loading states.
Shared heuristics infer role, tone, length, format, and constraints from raw prompt text, while scoring logic grades drafts before enhancement.
The service handles provider resolution, bearer-session validation, rate limiting, URL extraction, repository context, and SSE enhancement orchestration.
Neon Postgres via the Neon Data API provides authenticated persistence, while localStorage remains available for signed-out use.

keyFiles.note: Lazy-loaded route shell for builder, library, community, profiles, history, presets, and support.
Core prompt builder state management hook driving the builder workspace.
Shared heuristics that infer role, tone, format, and constraints from a draft prompt.
Main Codex agent service entry point with auth, rate limiting, and SSE streaming.
GitHub App context routes for installation, connection, file preview, and repository-backed builder context.
Freshness check that keeps documentation and linked references aligned with the shipped product surface.

ai-prompt-pro.shipped: Shipped Prompt Forge as a production prompt builder and community platform with an Azure-hosted enhancement service and Neon-backed persistence.
ai-prompt-pro.learned: The useful abstraction was not just prompt text; it was a durable prompt workspace with inference, persistence, community, and guarded enhancement boundaries.

flavor-agent.whyItMatters: Brings AI recommendations into Gutenberg so editors can stay in the flow instead of jumping to a separate tool.
problem: Editors needed block, pattern, template, template-part, and navigation help without leaving Gutenberg.
Recommendations needed retrieval and grounding instead of stuffing full pattern catalogs into one prompt.
Because the plugin touches editor UX and provider settings, validation and repeatable tests had to be built in.
approach: Mounted recommendation flows into five Gutenberg surfaces instead of sending users to a sidecar tool.
Shared the runtime through REST routes and 11 WordPress Abilities API capabilities.
Used Qdrant retrieval and Cloudflare AI Search grounding to keep recommendations scoped.
Added Docker, Playwright, PHPUnit, and Jest workflows around settings and editor flows.
result: The plugin ships five editor-facing recommendation surfaces with activity history and inline undo.
Pattern and template suggestions stay grounded before the model ranks or returns results.
Settings, recommendation paths, and editor flows ship with repeatable validation and test coverage.

receipts.label: Recommendation surfaces
receipts.value: Bootstrap wires the inspector, pattern, template, template-part, and navigation recommenders.

receipts.label: Grounded ranking
receipts.value: Pattern recommendations combine Qdrant retrieval, provider reranking, and Cloudflare AI Search grounding.

receipts.label: Verification loop
receipts.value: PHPUnit, Jest, and Playwright cover settings, REST paths, provider validation, and core editor flows.

architecture.summary: WordPress plugin with PHP settings and REST layers, JavaScript Gutenberg injections, and retrieval-backed AI services.
bullets: PHP bootstrap registers lifecycle hooks, settings, REST controllers, abilities, and warming jobs.
Editor-side JavaScript injects recommenders into Gutenberg surfaces instead of routing users to a separate app.
Qdrant retrieval and Cloudflare AI Search grounding shape suggestions before they reach the editor.

keyFiles.note: Plugin bootstrap for lifecycle hooks, settings, REST routes, abilities, and warming.
REST controller for block, pattern, template, template-part, and navigation recommendations.
Registers 11 Abilities API capabilities across editor and infrastructure features.
Cloudflare AI Search client for docs grounding, caching, and prewarm jobs.
Central data store for recommendation state, apply and undo, and activity history.
Playwright smoke coverage for apply and undo, pattern badges, and Site Editor template flows.

flavor-agent.shipped: Shipped a Gutenberg-native recommendation plugin with five editor surfaces, shared runtime capabilities, and inline undo history.
flavor-agent.learned: The hard part was making AI feel native to Gutenberg, with clear affordances, safe settings, and repeatable tests.

codex.whyItMatters: Useful as a fork-based systems case study: the authored work is narrow, but it sits inside a large Rust agent runtime where small state regressions are easy to miss.
problem: Rollout replay could overwrite a deliberately polluted thread memory mode with enabled state.
That downgrade risk lived inside low-level runtime metadata handling rather than a visible CLI surface.
Any fix needed to stay small, explicit, and regression-tested inside the fork without pretending authorship of the entire upstream product.
approach: Forked the open-source Codex repository and traced the issue to the Rust state runtime that persists thread metadata.
Added a guard in set_thread_memory_mode so rollout replay does not downgrade polluted memory mode back to enabled.
Locked the behavior with a focused async test around apply_rollout_items replay.
result: The fork now preserves polluted memory mode during rollout replay instead of silently downgrading thread state.
The change stays localized to the runtime state layer, which keeps the fix reviewable inside a much larger codebase.
The repository remains clearly an upstream fork; the value here is the targeted systems patch and regression coverage, not authorship of Codex as a whole.
highlights: Replay invariant around thread memory mode
Focused regression test in a large upstream fork

receipts.label: Runtime guard
receipts.value: threads.rs now short-circuits attempts to replace polluted memory mode with enabled during thread updates.

receipts.label: Regression coverage
receipts.value: A new async test asserts apply_rollout_items does not downgrade polluted memory mode to enabled during replay.

receipts.label: Upstream context
receipts.value: The wider repository still spans the Codex CLI, Rust runtime crates, npm wrapper, and CI workflows that this forked patch sits inside.

architecture.summary: Public fork of the Codex CLI and Rust runtime; this case study focuses on a targeted patch in codex-rs/state rather than the entire upstream system.
bullets: The upstream repository spans CLI tooling, Rust runtime crates, SDKs, package wrappers, and CI workflows.
The authored change lives in codex-rs/state runtime metadata handling for threads and rollout replay.
Keeping the patch narrow made it easier to reason about invariants inside a large agent codebase without forking the product direction itself.

keyFiles.note: Rust runtime file containing the memory-mode guard and the replay regression test.
Repository overview showing the broader CLI and runtime surface that the forked patch lives inside.
Rust CI workflow that frames the verification environment around the runtime crates.

codex.shipped: Patched my Codex fork so rollout replay preserves polluted memory mode and locked the behavior with a focused regression test.
codex.learned: In stateful agent runtimes, memory-mode transitions need explicit invariants; otherwise replay paths can silently erase intent.
codex.accessNote: This is a public fork of openai/codex. The case study covers the runtime-state work in my fork, not authorship of the upstream product.

ai-cli-web-funnel.whyItMatters: Replaces fragmented copy-paste CLI addon installation with a governed product flow spanning catalog discovery, dependency-aware install planning, runtime verification, security governance, profile bundles, and a user-facing CLI — modeled after CurseForge for CLI ecosystems.
problem: CLI addons (MCP servers, skills, plugins) were distributed through scattered docs with manual copy-paste installation and no central discovery.
There was no way to plan, verify, or roll back installs — teams had no visibility into what actually reached runtime.
Security and governance were afterthoughts: no trust gates, no audit trails, and no SLO measurement across the install funnel.
approach: Built a TypeScript monorepo (four apps, five packages) with a control-plane HTTP server, standalone runtime daemon, VS Code Copilot adapter, and a user-facing forge CLI.

Implemented a four-stage deterministic funnel — discover, plan, apply, verify — with update, remove, and rollback, idempotent replay, conflict detection, and append-only audit trails across 17 Postgres migrations.
Added hybrid retrieval (Postgres BM25 + Qdrant semantic search), outbox-driven event dispatch with dead-letter replay, security trust gates, SLO rollups, and profile bundle orchestration.
Made the runtime daemon a standalone process with its own HTTP API, process registry, stdio launcher, HTTP probe client (SSE + Streamable HTTP), Dockerfile, and docker-compose service.

Layered comprehensive testing: unit, integration, integration-db (Docker-provisioned Postgres), e2e, and contract tests, with CI covering typecheck, tests, governance drift, and migration verification.
result: Full install lifecycle shipped: discover → plan → apply → verify, plus update, remove, and rollback with idempotent replay semantics.
Security governance enforces trust gates, reporter scoring, and appeal workflows with deterministic SLO measurement across 10 metric families.
Profile bundles let users export, import, and batch-install curated addon sets with aggregate status tracking.
Standalone runtime daemon runs as its own process with HTTP API, process registry, health probes, and Docker support.
User-facing forge CLI wraps the control-plane API for search, plan, install, verify, update, remove, rollback, and profile operations.
Release candidate v0.2.0-rc.1 reached with fail-closed GA readiness gate, pilot-to-GA runbook, and gap assessment completed.
highlights: Four-stage install funnel (discover → plan → apply → verify)
Hybrid BM25 + semantic retrieval with graceful fallback
Outbox-driven event dispatch with dead-letter replay
Security trust gates and SLO rollups
Standalone runtime daemon with HTTP API and Docker support
Fail-closed GA readiness gate and pilot-to-GA runbook

receipts.label: CI baseline
receipts.value: Forge CI runs typecheck, unit/integration/contract tests, governance drift checks, and migration verification across the monorepo.

receipts.label: Retrieval stack
receipts.value: The control plane bootstraps BM25, Qdrant semantic search, and embedding-backed retrieval from a single bootstrap path.

receipts.label: GA readiness gate
receipts.value: Fail-closed GA readiness gate validates lifecycle, security, and operational criteria with a pilot-to-GA runbook and gap assessment.

architecture.summary: TypeScript monorepo with four apps (control plane, runtime daemon, forge CLI, VS Code adapter) and five packages backed by Postgres and Qdrant.
bullets: The control plane exposes v1 HTTP APIs for catalog search, install lifecycle (plan/apply/verify/update/remove/rollback), profiles, and security governance.
The standalone runtime daemon runs as its own process with an HTTP API (/v1/runtime/start, /verify, /stop, /status, /health), process registry, stdio launcher, and HTTP probe client.
The forge CLI wraps the control-plane API for end-user addon management (search, plan, install, verify, update, remove, rollback, profiles).

Five shared packages provide catalog connectors (GitHub, npm, PyPI, docs), hybrid retrieval ranking, security governance (trust gates, SLO rollups, outbox dispatch), policy engine, and typed contracts.
17 forward-only Postgres migrations enforce deterministic ordering, idempotent replay, and append-only audit across all lifecycle stages.

keyFiles.note: Control-plane entry point: HTTP APIs, startup validation, readiness checks, and install lifecycle orchestration.
Standalone runtime daemon entry point with HTTP server, process registry, and graceful shutdown.
Runtime daemon HTTP API for start, verify, stop, status, and health endpoints.
Core install lifecycle engine: plan, apply, verify, update, remove, and rollback with idempotent replay.
Deterministic SLO metric computation across 10 families including funnel KPIs and cold-start success rates.
Profile bundle API: create, export, import, and batch-install curated addon sets.

ai-cli-web-funnel.shipped: Reached release candidate v0.2.0-rc.1 with full install lifecycle, security governance, profile bundles, standalone runtime daemon, forge CLI, and fail-closed GA readiness gate.
ai-cli-web-funnel.learned: A strict four-stage funnel (discover → plan → apply → verify) with idempotent replay at every boundary kept the system predictable even as it grew to span four apps, five packages, security governance, profile orchestration, and outbox-driven event dispatch.

web-fetch.whyItMatters: LLM agents need safe, structured web content, and the hard part is normalizing real pages, enforcing security boundaries, and building a scoped knowledge base — not issuing HTTP requests.
problem: LLM agents fetching web content faced SSRF risks, prompt injection, and inconsistent formats that polluted context windows.
One-off fetch scripts could not be reused across agent runtimes or exposed as standardized tool surfaces.
Agents needed more than raw extraction: semantic chunking, intelligent compaction, and a conversation-scoped knowledge base for follow-up queries.
approach: Built an MCP server exposing five tools over stdio: fetch, extract, chunk, compact, and AI search query.
Added an SSRF guard that blocks private IPs and validates DNS, a prompt injection detector, a content sanitizer, and per-host rate limiting.
Separated HTTP fetching from six format-specific extractors (HTML, Markdown, PDF, JSON, XML, plain text) with optional Playwright rendering for SPAs.
Integrated Cloudflare AI Search for a conversation-scoped knowledge base that indexes fetched content and supports semantic follow-up queries.
Added intelligent compaction modes (structural, salience, map-reduce, question-focused) backed by Cloudflare AI Gateway.
result: Agents get safe, normalized web content from a standards-compliant MCP server instead of ad hoc scripts.
The security layer (SSRF guard, injection detection, rate limiting) makes the server suitable for use with untrusted URLs.
Fetched content automatically populates a scoped knowledge base that agents can query across turns without re-fetching.
The processing pipeline — extract, chunk, compact — lets agents control context size and relevance instead of dumping raw HTML into prompts.
highlights: Five MCP tools: fetch, extract, chunk, compact, AI search
SSRF guard with DNS rebinding prevention
Six format-specific extractors plus Playwright SPA rendering
Conversation-scoped Cloudflare AI Search knowledge base

receipts.label: MCP tool surface
receipts.value: The server registers five tools, six prompts, and a resource store with TTL caching over the MCP stdio transport.

receipts.label: Security boundary
receipts.value: The SSRF guard blocks private IPs, loopback, and link-local addresses, and validates DNS resolution before any fetch executes.

receipts.label: Test coverage
receipts.value: Twenty-three test files cover the full pipeline from fetch through extraction, chunking, compaction, and AI search integration.

architecture.summary: TypeScript MCP server with five tools, a security layer, six format extractors, semantic processing, and Cloudflare AI Search integration.
bullets: The MCP entrypoint registers fetch, extract, chunk, compact, and AI search query tools plus prompts, resource handlers, and completions.
A security layer enforces SSRF protection, prompt injection detection, content sanitization, and per-host rate limits before any content reaches the agent.
Six extractors normalize HTML, Markdown, PDF, JSON, XML, and plain text into a common LLMPacket format, with optional Playwright rendering for SPAs.
Cloudflare AI Search integration uploads fetched content to R2 and indexes it into a conversation-scoped knowledge base for semantic follow-up queries.

keyFiles.note: MCP server entrypoint registering all five tools, six prompts, resource handlers, and completions.
Core fetch tool: SSRF check, robots.txt, HTTP or Playwright fetch, extract, normalize, and AI Search ingest.
SSRF protection blocking private IPs and preventing DNS rebinding before any request executes.
Cloudflare R2 upload and AI Search query integration for the conversation-scoped knowledge base.
Semantic content chunking that respects heading and code block boundaries.

web-fetch.shipped: Shipped an MCP server for safe, structured web browsing with five tools, SSRF protection, and a conversation-scoped AI Search knowledge base.
web-fetch.learned: The hard part of web fetching for agents is not the HTTP request; it is the security boundary, format normalization, and the ability to build a scoped knowledge base that survives across turns.

tarot.whyItMatters: Demonstrates practical integration of AI interpretation into a consumer-facing interactive experience with clear UX affordances.
problem: Existing tarot apps offered static card lookups with no personalized context.
Users had no way to ask follow-up questions about their readings.
Card spread logic and AI interpretation were tightly coupled, making iteration slow.
approach: Built a JavaScript front end with modular spread layouts and card selection flows.
Integrated AI-powered interpretation that considers card position, orientation, and spread context.
Separated card data, spread logic, and interpretation prompts to allow independent iteration.
result: Readings feel personalized and contextual rather than generic card-definition lookups.
New spread types can be added without touching interpretation logic.
User engagement improved with interactive card selection and position-aware AI responses.

receipts.label: Reading flow coverage
receipts.value: Playwright covers the core journey from spread selection to reveal, narrative delivery, and save behavior.

receipts.label: Narrative backend
receipts.value: The reading function layers context inference, safety checks, entitlements, evaluation gates, and Azure Responses streaming into a single endpoint.

architecture.summary: Hybrid web/native tarot platform backed by Cloudflare functions and Azure OpenAI narrative services.
bullets: An Expo WebView shell reuses the deployed web experience for native mobile distribution.
Cloudflare function handlers assemble reading context, safety checks, entitlements, and streaming AI narration.
Node and Playwright suites protect the core reading, accessibility, and evaluation gates.

keyFiles.note: Expo shell that wraps the deployed web experience for native mobile delivery.
Primary reading endpoint that assembles context, safety, evaluation, and streaming response behavior.
Azure Responses configuration and request-normalization layer for the narrative backend.
High-value end-to-end coverage for the core reading journey and streamed narrative behavior.

tarot.shipped: Shipped a streaming tarot reading pipeline with Azure OpenAI narration, ritual UX, and end-to-end reading tests.
tarot.learned: Balancing ritual UX with explicit evaluation and safety gates made the app feel mystical without treating production quality as optional.

mystic-tarot.whyItMatters: Shows how much of a creative product comes from presentation, pacing, and persistence around the AI call, not just the generated text.
problem: A tarot app needs ceremony and visual identity, not just card lookup results.
Users needed a way to revisit readings instead of treating them as disposable outputs.
The reading backend had to support both AI-backed and local narrative paths.
approach: Built animated spread and reveal flows with celestial styling throughout the interface.
Implemented a reading endpoint that returns the narrative, provider used, and spread analysis metadata.
Added journal persistence through auth-backed API calls with a localStorage fallback when users are not signed in.
result: Readings feel more like a ritualized product experience than a form submission.
Saved entries and local fallbacks make the journal usable even when auth or API paths are limited.
The AI narration is request and response based rather than streamed, so the product story stays grounded in the actual implementation.
highlights: Azure or local reading provider path

receipts.label: Journal resilience
receipts.value: The journal hook saves through the API when authenticated and falls back to localStorage when it is not.

receipts.label: Reading payload
receipts.value: The reading endpoint returns the narrative, provider, themes, context, and spread-analysis metadata from one response.

architecture.summary: Front-end tarot experience backed by a reading endpoint and journal system with auth and local fallbacks.
bullets: React views handle spread selection, ritual states, card reveals, and formatted reading display.
API handlers assemble the reading response and report which provider path was used.
Journal utilities persist entries through authenticated routes or browser storage depending on session state.

keyFiles.note: Main reading experience with ritual state, reveal pacing, and reading presentation.
Card component used inside the animated spread and reveal flow.
Reading endpoint that returns the narrative, provider, and spread-analysis payload.
Journal persistence layer with authenticated and localStorage-backed paths.

mystic-tarot.shipped: Built an animated tarot reading experience with API-backed narratives and journal persistence.
mystic-tarot.learned: The memorable part of a creative AI app is often the pacing around the response, not the raw model call.

dj-judas.whyItMatters: Shows how a performer brand can be translated into a production web experience that combines storytelling, booking, and commerce rather than a static promo site.
problem: A performer site needed to do more than present bio copy; it had to support discovery, bookings, catalog browsing, and platform follow-through.
Mobile visitors needed a fast path from landing content to booking and streaming destinations.
Commerce and operational flows needed a backend that could grow beyond a brochure site.
approach: Built a React + TypeScript front end on Cloudflare Workers with SPA navigation, social integrations, and section-based landing experiences.
Implemented a mobile-friendly booking flow with persisted form state, validation, and submission support.
Integrated a Medusa backend and end-to-end test coverage for storefront and checkout-adjacent behavior.
result: The site now supports artist storytelling, booking intent capture, and product discovery in one surface.
Booking UX became more reliable on mobile thanks to local persistence and explicit validation states.
Commerce and support flows can evolve independently through the Medusa backend and dedicated E2E checks.

receipts.label: Booking flow
receipts.value: The booking form persists draft input locally, validates required fields, and submits through a dedicated request flow.

receipts.label: Release confidence
receipts.value: Playwright coverage and the e2e-tests workflow exercise checkout, social sharing, and platform integrations.

architecture.summary: React storefront on Cloudflare Workers with performer-focused landing sections, booking capture, and Medusa-backed commerce.
bullets: The SPA shell handles navigation, error boundaries, theme state, and social-provider initialization.
Landing components split the performer experience into hero, media, events, services, and booking sections.
A Medusa backend supports catalog and commerce workflows while E2E tests protect critical flows.

keyFiles.note: Top-level SPA shell for navigation, error boundaries, theme state, and social-provider setup.
Main landing experience that orchestrates hero, media, events, services, and booking sections.
Mobile-friendly inquiry form with validation, local persistence, and submission state handling.
Regression coverage for storefront checkout behavior alongside the rest of the performer experience.

dj-judas.shipped: Shipped a performer site with booking capture, social platform routing, and Medusa-backed commerce scaffolding.
dj-judas.learned: Combining artist storytelling, inquiry capture, and storefront behavior in one product highlighted where content design and operational UX need to meet.

image-studio.whyItMatters: Shows how one product can wrap multiple modern media-model workflows, from image generation and edits to vision analysis and Sora prompt creation.
problem: Image generation, editing, vision analysis, and video prompting were split across separate tools and inconsistent APIs.
Media workflows needed a reusable server boundary for Azure OpenAI and related model calls.
The UI needed to manage a growing local asset library while still showing progress and analytics around long-running tasks.
approach: Built a TypeScript server that wraps Azure OpenAI Responses API calls, image analysis schemas, and media-processing endpoints.
Added web components for generation, editing, image analysis, and Sora prompt and video flows on top of a shared API client.
Queued local analytics events and library actions so the browser app can track usage without blocking the core workflow.
result: Users can generate, edit, analyze, and remix media from one workspace instead of stitching together separate tools.
Vision analysis can feed directly into richer prompt-building for image or Sora flows.
The repo is strongest as a multi-workflow media studio; claims about generic chunked-upload infrastructure were overstated.
highlights: Azure OpenAI Responses and vision integrations
Local media library with queued analytics

receipts.label: Responses API wrapper
receipts.value: The server has a dedicated Azure Responses client that normalizes GPT-5 text and image-style requests.

receipts.label: Client telemetry
receipts.value: Analytics events are queued in localStorage and posted best-effort so UI tracking does not block the workflow.

architecture.summary: TypeScript media studio with a server-side model wrapper layer and a browser workspace for generation, analysis, and Sora flows.
bullets: Server libraries normalize Azure OpenAI Responses and vision requests behind shared configs and error handling.
Web components compose generation, editing, analysis, and Sora-specific experiences on top of the same API surface.
Client utilities manage local media state, analytics, and long-running progress feedback around model jobs.

keyFiles.note: Server entrypoint that exposes the media workflow APIs.
Azure Responses API wrapper used for GPT-5-backed generation workflows.
Vision analysis layer with structured outputs for media understanding.
Sora-specific client flow for reference analysis, prompt creation, and video generation.
Queued client analytics path that records media workflow events without blocking the UI.

image-studio.shipped: Built a TypeScript media studio that combines Azure OpenAI image workflows, vision analysis, and Sora creation in one app.
image-studio.learned: Once a product spans several media models, the real work is unifying request shapes and workflow state rather than adding yet another generation button.

my-mcp-github.whyItMatters: Demonstrates a practical MCP bridge from Azure AI Search into agent tooling, with multiple transports and runtime discovery instead of one custom connector.
problem: Agents needed a standard way to discover and invoke Azure AI Search capabilities without a bespoke integration per client.
Different clients wanted different transports: edge HTTP and SSE for remote use and stdio for local tooling.
The server also needed operational features like request correlation, logging, and Cloudflare runtime support.
approach: Built dynamic MCP tool families for indexes, documents, data sources, indexers, skillsets, services, and knowledge agents.
Implemented both Cloudflare Worker and Node stdio entrypoints so the same system can serve remote and local clients.
Configured Cloudflare runtime features such as Durable Objects, observability, tail consumers, and request IDs for debugging.
result: Clients can discover and invoke Azure AI Search operations through a single MCP surface.
HTTP, SSE, and stdio transports make the server usable across browser-like and local agent environments.
Operational visibility is stronger than a toy MCP demo because the runtime adds correlation IDs and Cloudflare observability support.

receipts.label: Transport coverage
receipts.value: The project ships a dynamic Cloudflare entrypoint and a separate Node stdio server for local MCP clients.

receipts.label: Edge ops support
receipts.value: The Worker runtime adds request IDs, SSE-safe headers, Durable Objects, and Cloudflare observability configuration.

architecture.summary: Azure AI Search MCP server with dynamic tool registration across Cloudflare edge and Node stdio runtimes.
bullets: Dynamic tool modules register search, document, datasource, indexer, skillset, and knowledge operations into the MCP surface.
Cloudflare entrypoints handle HTTP and SSE traffic, CORS, request correlation, and Worker runtime behavior.
Node stdio entrypoints support local MCP clients while sharing the same Azure Search tool context.

keyFiles.note: Cloudflare Worker entrypoint for dynamic MCP tools over HTTP and SSE.
Node stdio server entrypoint for local MCP client usage.
Cloudflare runtime configuration for Durable Objects, observability, and deployment behavior.
Automation path for syncing project assets into Azure-backed infrastructure.

my-mcp-github.shipped: Built an Azure AI Search MCP server with dynamic tools and both edge and stdio runtimes.
my-mcp-github.learned: MCP becomes much more useful once discovery, transport, and operations are treated as part of the product instead of as afterthoughts.

bookmark-extension.whyItMatters: Shows a pragmatic browser-extension approach to knowledge cleanup: scan, flag likely duplicates, and keep a human review step.
problem: Large bookmark libraries accumulate duplicates and inconsistent foldering faster than the native manager can handle.
Background scanning and cleanup jobs have to work within extension APIs and browser storage limits.
The review UI still needs to feel deliberate and accessible, not like a dump of raw similarity scores.
approach: Built a service-worker orchestration layer for scans, duplicate checks, imports, enrichment, and folder suggestions.
Implemented duplicate detection utilities and job-state persistence around the bookmark graph.
Added a typed React popup dashboard for review queues, job progress, metrics, and accessibility helpers.
result: Users get a review-driven cleanup flow instead of trying to manually diff huge bookmark trees.
Background jobs and stored queues make long-running cleanup work survive popup closures.
The product stays grounded in extension constraints by keeping the AI step as a signal inside a review workflow, not an automatic destructive action.

receipts.label: Job orchestration
receipts.value: The service worker wires scanning, dedupe, enrichment, import, and folder-suggestion jobs through browser storage and alarms.

receipts.label: Tooling baseline
receipts.value: The repo includes Node tests plus ESLint coverage across JavaScript and TypeScript popup code.

architecture.summary: Edge extension with a background job system and a typed popup UI for review-driven bookmark cleanup.
bullets: Service-worker code handles bookmark traversal, dedupe checks, queues, storage, notifications, and scheduled cleanup work.
Utility modules score likely duplicates and propose folder organization without collapsing the human review step.
Popup React components surface pending jobs, review queues, metrics, and accessibility-friendly controls.

keyFiles.note: Extension manifest defining permissions, runtime pieces, and popup wiring.
Background orchestration for bookmark scans, dedupe jobs, imports, and review queues.
Similarity-based duplicate detection utility used by the cleanup flow.
Typed React popup dashboard for job progress, review queues, and accessibility-aware controls.

bookmark-extension.shipped: Built an Edge bookmark-cleanup extension with background job orchestration and a typed review dashboard.
bookmark-extension.learned: For browser cleanup tools, the important product decision is not just detection quality; it is how much review friction you remove without taking unsafe actions.

rememberliterally.whyItMatters: Useful as an example of a chat app that keeps working across several storage and model configurations, even though the repo metadata drifted badly.
problem: Needed a lightweight web chat surface with optional user persistence rather than a fully managed auth platform.
The app had to support image uploads and model-selection controls from the same browser UI.
The backend needed to degrade gracefully when the database was unavailable.
approach: Built a Flask app with login and user registration flows and chat endpoints around shared OpenAI helper utilities.
Added optional SQLAlchemy persistence, but kept an in-memory fallback path when database setup fails.
Implemented a browser UI that stores username, theme, conversation state, selected model, and advanced options locally.
result: The app can still run as a demo even when database configuration is missing.
Users get model-selection and image-upload controls from the same simple chat surface.
The repo is clearly rough: the README and Bun scaffold describe a different project, so the case study has to follow the actual Flask implementation instead of the repository packaging.
highlights: Graceful fallback when DB setup fails

receipts.label: Fallback behavior
receipts.value: The Flask app marks the database unavailable and drops to in-memory storage instead of failing hard at startup.

receipts.label: Browser state handling
receipts.value: The client script stores conversation state, theme, selected model, and advanced options in localStorage while loading available deployments from the API.

architecture.summary: Flask chat application with optional database persistence, OpenAI helper utilities, and a stateful browser UI.
bullets: App routes handle user registration, chat requests, streaming placeholders, and model discovery.
SQLAlchemy-backed storage is optional, with fallback utilities used when the database path is unavailable.
Frontend scripts manage username, local conversation state, image uploads, model choice, and theme preferences.

keyFiles.note: Main Flask application for chat routes, persistence setup, and startup fallback behavior.
Helper module for AI response generation and related model integration.
HTML shell for the browser chat experience.
Client-side state management for theme, conversation history, uploads, and model selection.

rememberliterally.shipped: Built a Flask chat prototype with optional persistence, image upload, and configurable model controls.
rememberliterally.learned: This audit was a good reminder that repo names, READMEs, and scaffolds can drift far away from the code that actually runs.

simple.whyItMatters: Shows a more operational version of AI-assisted documentation generation, where extraction, orchestration, and monitoring are part of the same tool.
problem: Generating useful docs from Python code requires more than dumping source into a prompt.
The tool needed visibility into system behavior and metrics while processing large repositories.
The extraction layer had to preserve structural details like classes, functions, variables, and dependencies.
approach: Built a Python documentation generator around AST parsing, module extraction, and orchestrated doc generation.
Added dependency injection, monitoring, and metrics collection so long-running jobs can be inspected while they run.
Used typed models and exception flows throughout the core modules to keep the pipeline composable.
result: The repo can extract structured code data and hand it to a documentation orchestration pipeline with monitoring around the run.
Operational helpers make it easier to debug and observe the generator than a single-script proof of concept.
The manual smoke script and rough edges mean it still reads as internal tooling, but the implementation is clearly not a text-extraction utility.

receipts.label: Operational layer
receipts.value: The generator wires a system monitor and metrics collector into the documentation workflow instead of hiding runtime behavior.

receipts.label: Extraction depth
receipts.value: The code extractor parses modules into classes, functions, variables, constants, dependencies, and metrics.

architecture.summary: Python documentation pipeline that combines AST extraction, orchestration, and runtime monitoring.
bullets: Entry points configure dependencies, environment, and run-level monitoring before processing files.
Extraction modules parse Python source into structured elements and dependency information.
Documentation orchestrators consume that structured output while metrics and monitoring record the run.

keyFiles.note: CLI entrypoint that boots dependencies, monitoring, and the documentation run.
Configuration model for the documentation generator and its runtime behavior.
Monitoring and metrics support for long-running documentation jobs.
AST-based extraction layer that turns Python source into structured documentation inputs.

simple.shipped: Built an internal documentation generator with AST extraction and runtime monitoring around the workflow.
simple.learned: Instrumentation was as valuable as the generation logic itself because it exposed where the pipeline was actually spending time and failing.

wp-hperkins-com.whyItMatters: Shows the portfolio translated into a WordPress block theme without giving up structured data contracts, dynamic routes, or operational checks.
problem: The site needed a WordPress-native editing experience without collapsing the portfolio into generic templates.
Dynamic work and blog detail routes had to behave like the React source site while still living in WordPress.
A public theme repository needed fast smoke coverage because the site doubles as a live consulting surface.
approach: Built the henrys-digital-canvas block theme as a child of twentytwentyfive inside a broader WordPress repository.
Mapped the React source pages to 13 custom blocks, structured JSON data, and theme REST endpoints under /wp-json/henrys-digital-canvas/v1/.
Added rewrite handling for /work/{repo} and /blog/{slug}, plus Turnstile-backed contact submission and smoke automation.
result: The WordPress build preserves bespoke portfolio and blog flows while keeping editing inside Gutenberg.
Structured data contracts keep work, blog, resume, and contact blocks aligned across the theme.
Route, API, and browser smoke checks make live-site changes safer to ship.
highlights: Custom block theme for portfolio parity
Dynamic work and blog detail routes

receipts.label: Theme runtime
receipts.value: Theme bootstrap loads shared assets, block registration, and dynamic route handling for the portfolio theme.

receipts.label: Data contract layer
receipts.value: Theme REST routes expose structured content and Turnstile-verified contact handling under the henrys-digital-canvas namespace.

receipts.label: Deployment confidence
receipts.value: The smoke-check workflow runs route, API, and browser smoke suites against the live theme on push and on a daily schedule.

architecture.summary: Custom WordPress child theme and REST layer that mirror the React portfolio's content model inside Gutenberg.
bullets: The block theme and custom blocks recreate page-specific portfolio surfaces inside the WordPress editor instead of leaning on generic templates.
Rewrite and template logic support dynamic work and blog detail routes that match the React source site's URL model.
Server-rendered blocks like work-showcase combine local JSON contracts with GitHub proxy data, while the theme REST layer handles structured content and contact security.

keyFiles.note: Theme bootstrap for assets, block registration, and dynamic route setup inside the custom child theme.
Structured theme data contracts used to keep block inputs and front-end rendering predictable.
Theme REST layer for structured data delivery, contact handling, and Turnstile-backed verification.
Server-rendered work block that wires GitHub proxy endpoints to structured local case-study data.
Live-site smoke workflow covering route, API, and browser checks for the theme.

wp-hperkins-com.shipped: Shipped a public WordPress repository for the portfolio theme with custom blocks, dynamic routes, and live smoke automation.
wp-hperkins-com.learned: Replatforming into WordPress only worked when the block theme, structured contracts, and regression checks were treated as one system.

video_processing_toolkit.whyItMatters: Shows a practical multimodal CLI pipeline for turning raw video into tags and structured metadata.
problem: Manual review does not scale when you need descriptions, tags, and search-ready metadata across many videos.
A useful tool needed scene detection and model analysis, not just ffmpeg wrappers.
Results had to land in a structured format that downstream systems can ingest.
approach: Built a workflow script that downloads or loads video assets, extracts metadata, and runs scene detection.
Called Qwen2-VL for scene-level descriptions and paired that with tagging and classification utilities.
Exported the combined output as JSON so the toolkit can feed cataloging or retrieval systems.
result: Video processing becomes a repeatable CLI workflow instead of a manual inspection task.
Scene descriptions and tags make the output more useful for search and indexing than raw timestamps alone.
The repo is best described as a toolkit rather than a polished platform service.

receipts.label: Workflow orchestration
receipts.value: The main workflow coordinates ingest, metadata extraction, scene analysis, and export from one CLI path.

receipts.label: Model-backed tagging
receipts.value: Analysis and tagging modules turn scene outputs into descriptions, labels, and structured JSON records.

architecture.summary: CLI-first video analysis pipeline that chains scene detection, Qwen2-VL inference, tagging, and JSON export.
bullets: Workflow modules coordinate input handling, metadata extraction, and scene segmentation.
Model wrappers generate scene descriptions that feed tagging and classification utilities.
Export steps serialize the resulting metadata into JSON for downstream search or catalog flows.

keyFiles.note: Main CLI workflow that coordinates ingest, analysis, and export steps.
Analysis layer for scene processing and metadata derivation.
Qwen2-VL model wrapper used for multimodal scene understanding.
Tagging and classification utilities that shape the final output records.

video_processing_toolkit.shipped: Built a CLI toolkit for scene detection, Qwen2-VL analysis, tagging, and JSON export.
video_processing_toolkit.learned: For multimodal tooling, the downstream data contract matters as much as the model call because that is what makes the output reusable.
